clarifying_questions.why: Spec says printable ASCII. Unicode would complicate (multi-byte characters). Stay simple.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Two approaches: (1) filter the string to keep only alphanumerics and lowercase, then compare to its reverse — O(n) space. (2) Two pointers in-place from both ends, skipping non-alphanumerics on both sides — O(1) space. The in-place version is the senior answer.

edge_cases.details: ' ' (just space) → true. Same for '!!!' (only punctuation). Pointers immediately cross.
'a' → true. Single char is always a palindrome.

edge_cases.category: Digit vs letter at same position
edge_cases.details: '0P' → after cleaning '0p'. '0' != 'p' → false. The case-fold trap: don't accidentally compare uppercase-letter to digit.
'12321' → true. Confirms digits work as palindrome chars.
'!!!' → cleans to empty → true.

common_mistakes: Only skipping non-alphanumeric on ONE pointer (the other tries to compare punctuation).
Forgetting case-folding before comparison — 'Aa' fails when it should pass.
Off-by-one when pointers cross — comparing too far.
Using regex to clean (works but slow and uses extra memory).
Returning False on first non-letter — wrong. Just skip non-alphanumerics; don't fail on them.

faang.introduction: Classic string two-pointer problem. Given a string, return true if it's a palindrome (ignoring case and non-alphanumeric chars).
faang.introduction_context: Easy difficulty but the senior signal is the in-place O(1) space approach. Sets up follow-ups like 'Valid Palindrome II' (allow one mismatch) or 'Longest Palindromic Substring'.
really_testing: Two-pointer-from-ends pattern recognition.
Space optimization: filter-then-compare vs in-place pointers.
Edge case handling: empty cleaned string, case folding, the '0P' digit-letter trap.
Clean code with proper alphanumeric checks and case folding.

rubric.criteria: Proposes in-place two-pointer immediately. Skips non-alphanumeric on BOTH sides. Handles case correctly. Tests on '0P' and pure punctuation. ~6 min.
Implements filter-then-compare correctly. Reaches in-place with a small nudge. Tests well.
Buggy two-pointer (only skips on one side, or off-by-one). Fails the '0P' trap. Forgets case-folding.
Can't reach a correct solution. Mishandles non-alphanumeric. Doesn't recognize two-pointer pattern.

playbook: Restate: 'Palindrome — same forward and backward — ignoring case and non-alphanumeric.'
Ask: 'Are digits alphanumeric?' (yes) and 'Empty string after cleaning?' (yes, palindrome).
State two approaches: filter-then-compare (O(n) space) and two-pointer in-place (O(1) space).
Pick the in-place. Senior interviewers expect it.

Code: left=0, right=len-1. While left<right: skip non-alphanumerics on left (advance left); skip non-alphanumerics on right (retreat right); if s[left].lower() != s[right].lower(), return False; advance both.
Trace through 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama' for one or two steps to demonstrate.
Test on '0P' — confirms case-folding doesn't accidentally match digit with letter.
Test on ' ' — confirms empty-after-cleaning works.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 6-8 min for a strong run. Common follow-up: Valid Palindrome II (allow 1 mismatch — DP-flavored).
dont_do: Don't forget to skip non-alphanumeric on BOTH pointers — common bug.
Don't forget case-folding before comparison.
Don't return False on a non-letter character — that's just a skip, not a fail.
Don't use regex if you can avoid it — slower and not the senior signal.
Don't compare a non-alphanumeric to another character — skip it first.

approaches.name: Filter and Compare
approaches.explanation: Build a filtered, lowercased list of alphanumeric characters. Compare to its reverse. Simple to write and correct, but uses O(n) extra space.

approaches.name: Two Pointers In-Place
approaches.explanation: Two pointers from the ends. Skip non-alphanumerics on each side independently. Lowercase-compare. If any pair mismatches, fail. If pointers cross without failure, success. O(1) extra space — the senior answer.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
summary: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without duplicate characters.
constraints: s consists of English letters, digits, symbols and spaces.

examples.explanation: The answer is "abc", with the length of 3.
The answer is "b", with the length of 1.
The answer is "wke". Note: "pwke" is a subsequence, not a substring.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Sliding window with a set or map. Expand the right end of the window. When a duplicate appears, shrink the left end past it. Track the max window size. The map-based version with last-index lookup skips left more aggressively in one step.

clarifying_questions.question: Substring or subsequence?
clarifying_questions.why: Substring = contiguous. Subsequence = not necessarily contiguous. Big difference. Spec says substring. 'pwke' from 'pwwkew' is a subsequence, NOT a substring.

clarifying_questions.question: Are characters ASCII or Unicode?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says English letters, digits, symbols, spaces. So ASCII printables. A fixed-size int[128] could replace the hash map for O(1) space (constant alphabet).

clarifying_questions.question: What's the size of s?
clarifying_questions.why: Up to 5×10^4. O(n) is required; O(n²) is borderline. Sliding window is O(n).

clarifying_questions.question: Should the answer be the length or the substring itself?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says length (an int). Some variants ask for the substring. Always confirm.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty string?
clarifying_questions.why: Returns 0. Trivial edge case but worth confirming.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Brute force: try every substring, check uniqueness — O(n³). State and skip. Sliding window: maintain [left, right] window with all unique chars. Walk right forward. When s[right] is already in the window, advance left past the duplicate. Update max length each step. Use a set for membership OR a map of char→last_index (lets left jump directly).

edge_cases.details: "" → 0. Loop doesn't execute, return 0.
"a" → 1. Window of size 1.
"bbbb" → 1. Window can never grow past 1.
"abcdef" → 6. Window grows to full length.

edge_cases.category: Duplicate forces big jump (use map version)
edge_cases.details: "abba". After 'a' at 0, 'b' at 1, 'b' at 2: dup! Move left to 2 (past the first 'b'). Then 'a' at 3: dup with 'a' at 0, but left is already at 2. CRITICAL: only jump left forward, never backward. The map-based version needs `left = max(left, map[c] + 1)`.

common_mistakes: Moving left BACKWARD when handling a duplicate (map version) — must take max with current left.
Forgetting to update max length on the final window after the loop ends.
Using s.find() / s.index() to locate the duplicate — O(n) per lookup makes the whole thing O(n²).
Treating 'subsequence' and 'substring' interchangeably — substring is contiguous.
Off-by-one on window length: right - left + 1 (inclusive) when both pointers point at chars.

faang.introduction: Classic sliding window. Given a string, find the length of the longest substring with all unique characters.
faang.introduction_context: Universal interview question. Tests the sliding window pattern at its purest. Often the first 'sliding window' problem candidates encounter in a loop.
really_testing: Recognition that 'contiguous + constraint' = sliding window.
Comfort with the expand-right-shrink-left pattern.
Choosing the right data structure for membership/last-index: set vs map.
Handling the 'don't move left backward' subtlety in the map-based version.

rubric.criteria: Recognizes sliding window immediately. Picks set-based approach for simplicity. Implements correctly. Traces through 'pwwkew' to verify. Mentions the map-based optimization. ~10 min.
Reaches sliding window after stating brute force. Set-based works. May not mention the map optimization.
Tries brute force O(n²) with substring extraction. Or implements map-based but moves left backward (introduces bug on inputs like 'abba').
Can't recognize the sliding window pattern. Doesn't reach O(n).

playbook: Restate: 'Longest substring (contiguous) without repeating characters.'
Ask about ASCII vs Unicode and confirm substring (not subsequence).
State brute force: every (i,j) substring, check uniqueness. O(n³). Don't code.
Propose sliding window with a set.
Code: left=0, max_len=0, seen=set(). For right in range(n): while s[right] in seen, remove s[left] and left++. Then add s[right]. Update max_len.
Trace through 'pwwkew' to verify the window mechanics.
Mention the map-based optimization: instead of advancing left one-at-a-time, jump it past the duplicate's previous index. Faster in practice.
Highlight the 'left = max(left, map[c] + 1)' subtlety to avoid moving left backward.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 10-15 min. Common follow-ups: longest substring with K distinct chars (parameterized), longest repeating character replacement (variable window).
dont_do: Don't use string.find() to locate duplicates inside the window — O(n) per lookup makes the whole thing O(n²).
Don't move left backward in the map-based version — bug on inputs like 'abba'.
Don't confuse substring with subsequence.
Don't reset the entire window when a duplicate is found — only shrink past the duplicate.
Don't forget to update max length BEFORE the loop ends (or after — but be consistent).

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every substring, check uniqueness via set. Correct but O(n³) — too slow.

approaches.name: Sliding Window with Set
approaches.code:
def length_of_longest_substring(s):
    seen = set()
    left = 0
    best = 0
    for right in range(len(s)):
        while s[right] in seen:
            seen.remove(s[left])
            left += 1
        seen.add(s[right])
        best = max(best, right - left + 1)
    return best
approaches.explanation: Maintain a window [left, right] of unique chars. When s[right] is already in the window, shrink from left until the duplicate is removed. Each character is added once and removed at most once — O(n) total.

approaches.name: Sliding Window with Map (Optimization)
approaches.explanation: Instead of incrementing left one step at a time, jump it directly past the duplicate's last position. The `max(left, ...)` guard is critical — prevents left from moving backward (which would happen on inputs like 'abba' where 'a' was seen at index 0 but left has already advanced to 2). Strictly faster in practice but same asymptotic complexity.

title: Minimum Window Substring
summary: Given two strings s and t of lengths m and n respectively, return the minimum window substring of s such that every character in t (including duplicates) is included in the window. If there is no such substring, return the empty string.
constraints: s and t consist of uppercase and lowercase English letters.

examples.explanation: The minimum window 'BANC' contains 'A', 'B', and 'C' from string t.
Entire string is the window.
Both 'a's from t must be in the window. s has only one 'a' — no valid window.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Variable-size sliding window. Expand right until the window contains all characters of t (with correct frequencies). Then SHRINK from the left while maintaining the constraint. Track the smallest valid window seen. Use a character-count map of t and a 'matched count' to know when the window is valid.

clarifying_questions.question: Are characters case-sensitive?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says both uppercase and lowercase. So yes — 'A' and 'a' are different. Worth confirming.

clarifying_questions.question: Do duplicate chars in t need to be matched?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. If t = 'aab', window must contain at least 2 'a's and 1 'b'. Frequency matters, not just presence.

clarifying_questions.question: What if multiple windows have the same min length?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec doesn't specify — return any. Most implementations return the first one found.

clarifying_questions.question: Length constraints?
clarifying_questions.why: Both up to 10^5. O(n) sliding window is required; O(n²) brute force times out.

clarifying_questions.question: What if there's no valid window?
clarifying_questions.why: Return empty string. Worth confirming the sentinel.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Use a character-frequency map for t. Use a running map for the window. Track 'matched' — how many DISTINCT characters of t are currently fully satisfied in the window (i.e., window[c] >= t[c]). Expand right: increment window[s[right]], if it now satisfies t[s[right]], increment matched. When matched == number of unique chars in t, try to shrink from left: as long as matched stays maximal, advance left and update the best window. When matched drops, expand right again.

edge_cases.details: s='a', t='aa' → no valid window → ''.
s='abc', t='abc' → the whole string is the answer.
t='aab' requires 2 'a's in the window. A window with just 1 'a' isn't valid.

edge_cases.category: Window must include all of t somewhere
edge_cases.details: s='AXXXBC', t='ABC' → 'AXXXBC' is the answer (no shorter window exists).
Per constraints, both have length ≥ 1.

common_mistakes: Tracking only chars in t (ignoring extras in s) but forgetting that extras don't violate the constraint — only missing chars do.
Forgetting that DUPLICATES matter — 'aab' is not satisfied by a window with just one 'a'.
Shrinking left too aggressively — must STOP shrinking when matched drops below complete.
Off-by-one on the best window: store (left, right) bounds, not the substring (saves O(m) substring copies in the inner loop).
Using a hash set instead of a counter for t — misses duplicates.

faang.introduction: Hard sliding window. Given strings s and t, find the smallest substring of s that contains every character of t (with correct frequencies).
faang.introduction_context: Classic FAANG hard. Tests advanced sliding window technique (variable size, with frequency constraints). Considered the canonical 'hard sliding window' problem.
really_testing: Variable-size sliding window pattern (expand to satisfy, shrink to optimize).
Frequency-tracking via Counter / dict.
Maintaining a 'matched count' to know when the window is valid in O(1) per step.
Distinguishing 'matched all chars of t' from 'every char in window is in t' (it's the FORMER).

rubric.criteria: Articulates the expand-then-shrink pattern. Maintains a 'matched' count for O(1) window-validity check. Tracks the best as (left, right) indices, not substring copies. ~20 min.
Reaches the sliding-window approach. May copy substrings in the inner loop (O(n) per copy, total O(n²)). Correctness achieved.
Stuck at brute force O(n² × m). Or doesn't handle frequency correctly (treats t as a set).
Can't reach a working sliding window. Doesn't recognize the pattern.

playbook: Restate: 'find smallest substring of s containing all chars of t with frequencies'.
Confirm: case-sensitive, duplicates in t count separately.
State brute force: every (i,j) substring, check if it satisfies t. O(n² × m).
Propose sliding window. Use Counter for t. Track window counter + matched count.
Expand right: increment window[c]. If window[c] == t[c], matched++.
Shrink while matched == len(t Counter): track best (left, right); decrement window[s[left]]; if window[c] < t[c], matched--; advance left.
Code carefully — the matched check is the trickiest part.
Walk through 'ADOBECODEBANC' with t='ABC'. Find 'ADOBEC' first, then shrink to 'BEC' (insufficient), expand again...
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 18-25 min. Hard but a clear sliding window. Common follow-up: minimum window with at most K distinct characters.
dont_do: Don't use a set for t — duplicates would be lost.
Don't shrink past the matched-complete state — once matched drops, you've gone too far.
Don't copy substrings in the inner loop. Track (left, right) bounds.
Don't treat extras in s as violations — only missing chars matter.
Don't return None or null — return empty string when no window exists.

approaches.name: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every (i, j) substring, check if it contains t. Quadratic. Won't pass for n=10^5.

approaches.name: Sliding Window with Matched Counter
approaches.explanation: Maintain a window and a 'matched' counter (how many of t's unique characters are fully satisfied). Expand right: update window counter; if a char just hit its required count, matched++. When matched == required, shrink left to find the minimum window starting at this right. As long as matched stays at required, advance left. When matched drops, resume expanding right. O(n) total because each char is added and removed at most once.

title: Binary Search
summary: Given an array of integers nums which is sorted in ascending order, and an integer target, write a function to search target in nums. If target exists, then return its index. Otherwise, return -1. You must write an algorithm with O(log n) runtime complexity.
constraints: All the integers in nums are unique.
nums is sorted in ascending order.

examples.explanation: 9 exists at index 4.
2 does not exist in nums.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Sorted array means binary search. Maintain a [left, right] bracket. Compute mid. If nums[mid] == target, return mid. If nums[mid] < target, search the right half (left = mid+1). Else search the left half (right = mid-1). Repeat until brackets cross. Watch your bracket invariants — they're the #1 source of binary search bugs.

clarifying_questions.question: Is the array sorted? In what order?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says ascending. Confirms binary search is applicable. Different sort orders (descending, rotated) need adjusted comparisons.

clarifying_questions.question: Are there duplicates?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says unique. If duplicates existed, you'd need to decide: return any match, leftmost, rightmost? Different variants (lower_bound vs upper_bound).

clarifying_questions.question: What should I return if target not found?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says -1. Other variants return insertion point. Always confirm.

clarifying_questions.question: Iterative or recursive — preference?
clarifying_questions.why: Iterative is generally preferred (no stack overhead, easier to reason about edge cases). State your default but offer either.

clarifying_questions.question: Watch for integer overflow on mid calculation?
clarifying_questions.why: (left + right) / 2 can overflow for very large arrays. left + (right - left) / 2 is safer. n ≤ 10^4 here, so doesn't matter, but show you know the trick.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: State the invariant: 'target, if it exists, is in nums[left..right] inclusive'. At each step, look at mid. Update left or right to keep the invariant. Terminate when left > right (target not found) or when nums[mid] == target (return mid).

edge_cases.details: [1] target 1 → 0. Confirm your loop runs at least once when left == right.
[1] target 0 → -1. Confirm termination when left > right.
[1,2,3] target 1 → 0; target 3 → 2. Both extremes should work without off-by-one.
[1,2,3] target 0 or target 10 → -1. Binary search should terminate quickly.
[2,5] target 5 → 1. Test the smallest non-trivial case carefully.

common_mistakes: Off-by-one in bracket update: writing `left = mid` (not mid+1) causes infinite loop when target is right of mid.
Wrong termination: `while left < right` vs `while left <= right` — must be careful which one matches your bracket semantics.
Using (left + right) // 2 — risks integer overflow for huge arrays. Use left + (right - left) // 2 for safety.
Forgetting the `return -1` after the loop — if loop exits without finding, must return the sentinel.
Recursive version with no base case — infinite recursion on miss.

faang.introduction: Binary search on a sorted array. Find the index of target, or return -1.
faang.introduction_context: Baseline binary search. Tests whether you can write the loop without off-by-one bugs. Foundation for harder variants (rotated arrays, binary search on answer).
really_testing: Can you write binary search without bugs on the first try?
Bracket invariant discipline ([left, right] inclusive vs [left, right) exclusive).
Awareness of integer overflow on mid calculation.
Termination correctness (no infinite loops, no missed elements).

rubric.criteria: Writes iterative binary search correctly on first attempt. States the invariant aloud. Mentions overflow-safe mid. Tests on edge cases (single element, target at boundary). ~5 min.
Writes binary search correctly. May have a small off-by-one initially but catches it during testing. Code is readable.
Linear scan instead of binary search. Or binary search with an off-by-one that causes infinite loop or missed elements.
Can't write binary search at all. Or writes a fundamentally broken version (e.g., always updates only left).

playbook: Restate. Confirm sorted ascending and unique elements.
State the invariant: 'target is in nums[left..right] inclusive if it exists'.
Code iteratively: left = 0, right = n-1. While left <= right: mid = (left + right) // 2; compare nums[mid] to target; update left or right.
On nums[mid] < target → left = mid + 1 (search right half).
On nums[mid] > target → right = mid - 1 (search left half).
On nums[mid] == target → return mid.
After loop, return -1.
Walk through example [-1,0,3,5,9,12] target=9 step by step.
Test on edge cases: single element, target at first/last index, target absent.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 5-7 min for a strong run. Common follow-ups: Search in Rotated Sorted Array, Find Min in Rotated Sorted Array, Search Insert Position.
dont_do: Don't use linear scan when binary search is asked for — defeats the point.
Don't write `left = mid` (without the +1) — causes infinite loops.
Don't use `while left < right` carelessly — it can miss the target when left == right.
Don't forget to return -1 after the loop.
Don't use recursion for a simple binary search — iterative is cleaner and avoids stack overhead.

approaches.name: Linear Scan (Wrong Tool)
approaches.explanation: Linear scan. Works correctly but ignores the sorted property — wastes the structure of the input. Wouldn't pass the 'O(log n) runtime' requirement in the spec.

approaches.name: Iterative Binary Search
approaches.explanation: Classic binary search. Maintain the invariant: target, if it exists, is in nums[left..right] (inclusive). Halve the search space each iteration. Total O(log n). The overflow-safe mid calculation is `left + (right - left) // 2`.

approaches.name: Recursive Binary Search
approaches.explanation: Same algorithm, recursive form. Slightly cleaner to read but uses O(log n) stack space. Generally interviewers prefer iterative for binary search.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
summary: There is an integer array nums sorted in ascending order (with distinct values). Prior to being passed, nums is possibly rotated at an unknown pivot index k. Given the array nums after the rotation and an integer target, return the index of target if it is in nums, or -1 if it is not in nums. You must write an algorithm with O(log n) runtime complexity.
constraints: All values of nums are unique.
nums is guaranteed to be rotated at some pivot.

examples.explanation: 0 exists at index 4.
3 is not in nums.
Single element, target not present.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: The array is sorted, then rotated at some unknown pivot. The KEY insight: at any midpoint, ONE side (left of mid OR right of mid) is fully sorted in ascending order. You can detect which side is sorted by comparing nums[left] to nums[mid]. Once you know the sorted side, check if target falls in that range. If yes, search there. If no, search the other (rotated) side.

clarifying_questions.question: Are there duplicates in the array?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says unique. THIS IS CRITICAL — duplicates change the algorithm fundamentally (cannot reliably detect the sorted side; needs the 'Search in Rotated Sorted Array II' variant).

clarifying_questions.question: Is the rotation guaranteed (non-zero pivot)?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says 'possibly rotated' and 'guaranteed to be rotated at some pivot'. If pivot=0, the array is just sorted normally — our algorithm should handle that too.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the size of n?
clarifying_questions.why: Up to 5000. O(n) linear scan would actually pass, but the spec says O(log n) — must do modified binary search.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I return -1 or some other sentinel if not found?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says -1. Confirm — variants sometimes return the insertion position.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the rotation point coincide with target?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. E.g., nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], target=7 → return 3. The pivot is between 7 and 0, but 7 is still findable.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: State the invariant: at any binary-search midpoint, ONE side is fully sorted. Specifically: nums[left] <= nums[mid] means the left half is sorted; otherwise, the right half is sorted. Once I know which side is sorted, I check if target is in that side's range. If yes, search there. If no, search the OTHER half (the rotated one). Each iteration halves the search space → O(log n).

edge_cases.details: [1], target=1 → 0.
[1,2,3], target=2 → 1. The 'left half sorted' branch handles this naturally.
[4,5,6,7,0,1,2], target=7 → 3 or target=0 → 4. Both straddle the pivot — algorithm must handle.

edge_cases.category: Target less than first element of left-sorted half
edge_cases.details: [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], target=2. nums[left]=4, nums[mid]=7. Left half [4,5,6,7] is sorted. target=2 is NOT in [4,7], so search right half [0,1,2].

common_mistakes: Using <= vs < incorrectly when checking 'is left half sorted' — must be nums[left] <= nums[mid].
Forgetting to compare target to the RANGE (e.g., nums[left] <= target < nums[mid]) before searching that half.
Treating the right half as sorted without checking — sometimes BOTH halves can have a complex layout when n is small.
Off-by-one in mid update: must use left = mid + 1 or right = mid - 1 (not left = mid).
Using recursion without a base case — infinite loop when target isn't found.

faang.introduction: Modified binary search. Array is sorted ascending then rotated at unknown pivot. Find target in O(log n).
faang.introduction_context: Classic FAANG question testing whether you can adapt binary search to a non-standard sorted structure. Very common at Google, Meta, Amazon.
really_testing: Recognition that this is a modified binary search problem.
Discovery of the invariant: 'one half is always sorted'.
Implementation discipline (off-by-one, range comparisons, bracket semantics).
Awareness that duplicates would change the algorithm — proves you read constraints.

rubric.criteria: Recognizes modified binary search. Articulates 'one half is sorted' invariant. Codes correctly first try. Tests on edge cases including target straddling the pivot. ~15 min.
Reaches the algorithm with one or two hints. Initial version has a small bug that's fixed on testing. Total time ~20 min.
Linear scans (defeats the O(log n) requirement). Or tries to find pivot first then binary-search (works but two-pass). Or buggy half-detection.
Can't reach O(log n). Doesn't recognize that the binary-search pattern is adaptable.

playbook: Restate. Confirm: unique values, definitely rotated (pivot may be 0), return -1 on miss.
State linear scan O(n) and acknowledge it doesn't meet O(log n) spec.
Propose modified binary search. Highlight the key invariant: 'at any midpoint, one half is sorted in ascending order.'
Code: left=0, right=n-1. While left<=right: mid=(left+right)//2; if nums[mid]==target return mid.
Detect which half is sorted: if nums[left] <= nums[mid], left half is sorted. Else right half is sorted.
Check if target is in the sorted half's range. If yes, recurse there. If no, recurse other half.

Walk through [4,5,6,7,0,1,2] target=0: mid=3 (nums=7), nums[left]=4<=7 → left half sorted ([4,7]). target=0 not in [4,7] → go right. left=4, right=6, mid=5 (nums=1), nums[left]=0<=1 → left half [0,1] sorted. target=0 in [0,1] → go left. left=4, right=4, mid=4 (nums=0) → found!
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-18 min. Common follow-ups: Search in Rotated Sorted Array II (with duplicates), Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array.
dont_do: Don't linear scan when O(log n) is required.
Don't find the pivot first then binary-search — works but two-pass when one-pass suffices.
Don't use nums[left] < nums[mid] (strict) — use <= to handle left == mid correctly.
Don't forget to clamp range checks: target must be both ≥ nums[left] AND < nums[mid] to be in the left half.
Don't assume nums[left] is the smallest element — after rotation, it might be the largest of the left half but not globally.

approaches.name: Linear Scan
approaches.explanation: Linear scan. Correct but ignores the structure of the input and fails the O(log n) requirement.

approaches.name: Modified Binary Search
approaches.explanation: Adapted binary search. At each midpoint, one half is fully sorted — we can tell by comparing nums[left] to nums[mid]. Check if target falls in the sorted half's range; if yes, recurse there; if not, recurse the other half. Each iteration halves the search space → O(log n).

approaches.name: Find Pivot Then Binary Search (Two-Pass)
approaches.explanation: Two-pass: first find the pivot index (start of the rotated sequence), then standard binary-search the appropriate half. Cleaner separation of concerns but two passes instead of one. Same asymptotic complexity. Useful if you can't fit the one-pass invariant in your head.

title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
summary: Suppose an array of length n sorted in ascending order is rotated between 1 and n times. Given the sorted rotated array nums of unique elements, return the minimum element of this array. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(log n) time.
constraints: All the integers of nums are unique.
nums is sorted and rotated between 1 and n times.

examples.explanation: Original array was [1,2,3,4,5], rotated 3 times.
Original array was [0,1,2,4,5,6,7], rotated 4 times.
Original array was [11,13,15,17], rotated 4 times (back to original).

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Binary search variant. The minimum is the pivot point of the rotation. KEY INSIGHT: at any midpoint, comparing nums[mid] to nums[right] tells you which side the minimum is on. If nums[mid] > nums[right], the minimum is in the right half (the array dipped after mid). Else, the minimum is in the left half (including mid).

clarifying_questions.question: Are there duplicates?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says unique. If duplicates existed, you'd need 'Find Min II' variant — duplicates can make nums[mid] == nums[right], requiring a O(n) worst case.

clarifying_questions.question: Is the array always rotated, or could rotation be 0?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says rotated between 1 and n times. Rotated n times == original. So a 'sorted' array IS a valid rotated input, and the answer is nums[0].

clarifying_questions.question: What's the size of n?
clarifying_questions.why: Up to 5000. O(log n) required by spec. O(n) linear scan would actually pass at 5000 but violates spec.

clarifying_questions.question: Compare to mid+1 or to right?
clarifying_questions.why: Either works. nums[mid] > nums[right] is more common because nums[right] doesn't need a bounds check (whereas nums[mid+1] does).

clarifying_questions.question: Return the value or the index?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says return the value (the minimum element). Worth confirming.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: The rotation pivot IS the minimum. Binary search: maintain [left, right]. At each mid, ask: 'Is the minimum to my LEFT or RIGHT?' If nums[mid] > nums[right], the array dipped after mid → minimum is in (mid, right]. If nums[mid] < nums[right], the right half is sorted ascending → minimum is in [left, mid] (could be mid itself). Halve the range each step. Terminate when left == right — that's the minimum.

edge_cases.details: [1,2,3,4,5] → 1. The array is fully sorted. nums[mid] < nums[right] always → search left. Eventually left lands at 0.
[5] → 5. Loop doesn't execute, return nums[0].
[2,1] → 1. mid=0, nums[0]=2 > nums[1]=1 → search right. left=right=1, return nums[1].
[1,2,3,4,5,6] → 1 (no real rotation) or [6,1,2,3,4,5] → 1 (rotated by 1).

edge_cases.category: Minimum at the very end of left half
edge_cases.details: [3,4,5,1,2] → 1. The minimum is at index 3.

common_mistakes: Comparing nums[mid] to nums[mid+1] without bounds-checking — risk of IndexError on the last mid.
Off-by-one in pointer updates: left = mid + 1 vs left = mid affect termination.
Returning nums[mid] when the loop ends — should return nums[left] (the converged position).
Using nums[mid] vs nums[left] for comparison — only nums[mid] vs nums[right] is the canonical pattern here.
Assuming the minimum is always at the rotation point — true, but the algorithm must FIND it.

faang.introduction: Modified binary search. Find the minimum element of a sorted-then-rotated array in O(log n).
faang.introduction_context: Often paired with Search in Rotated Sorted Array. Tests the same 'one half is sorted' invariant but applied to finding an extreme rather than a target.
really_testing: Adaptation of binary search to a non-monotonic-but-rotated array.
Discovery of the nums[mid] vs nums[right] invariant.
Implementation discipline (off-by-one, bracket invariants).
Edge case handling (no rotation, single element).

rubric.criteria: Reaches the binary search variant directly. Articulates the nums[mid] vs nums[right] invariant. Codes correctly first try. ~10 min.
Reaches O(log n) after some thought. Maybe initially compares wrong indices; catches it during testing.
Linear scan O(n). Or buggy binary search (e.g., off-by-one infinite loop).
Can't reach O(log n). Doesn't recognize the binary search adaptation.

playbook: Restate. Confirm uniqueness, rotation guarantee, O(log n) required.
State linear scan O(n), acknowledge it fails the spec.
Propose binary search with the nums[mid] vs nums[right] invariant.
Articulate: 'If nums[mid] > nums[right], the array dipped after mid → minimum is in (mid, right]. Else the right half is sorted → minimum is in [left, mid].'
Code: left=0, right=n-1. While left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] > nums[right]: left = mid+1; else: right = mid. Return nums[left].
Note: termination is left == right (not left <= right) — we want to converge on a single index.
Walk through [4,5,6,7,0,1,2]: mid=3, nums=7>2 → left=4. mid=5, nums=1<2 → right=5. mid=4, nums=0<1 → right=4. left==right==4, return nums[4]=0.
Test on no-rotation [1,2,3] → 1.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Variant of Search in Rotated Sorted Array. Common follow-up: Find Min II (with duplicates).
dont_do: Don't linear scan when O(log n) is required.
Don't compare nums[mid] to nums[mid+1] — risk IndexError, and not the cleanest invariant.
Don't use left <= right — termination gets harder.
Don't use left = mid (without +1) — risks infinite loop.
Don't return nums[mid] at the end — return nums[left] after convergence.

approaches.name: Linear Scan
approaches.code:
def find_min(nums):
    return min(nums)
approaches.explanation: Trivial O(n) approach. Correct but fails the O(log n) spec requirement.

approaches.name: Binary Search
approaches.explanation: Compare nums[mid] to nums[right]. If nums[mid] > nums[right], there must be a 'dip' between mid and right (since a sorted ascending array would have nums[mid] ≤ nums[right]) — the minimum is in (mid, right]. Otherwise, the right half from mid to right is monotonically increasing, so the minimum is in [left, mid] (and mid could itself be the minimum). Use `right = mid` (not mid-1) to keep mid as a candidate. Terminate when left == right — that's the minimum.

title: Koko Eating Bananas
summary: Koko loves bananas. There are n piles, the ith pile has piles[i] bananas. The guards return in h hours. Koko picks an eating speed k bananas/hour. Each hour she picks a pile and eats k bananas (or the rest if pile < k, then nothing else that hour). Return the MINIMUM k such that she can eat all bananas in h hours.

examples.explanation: At speed 4: pile 3 takes 1 hour, pile 6 takes 2 hours, pile 7 takes 2 hours, pile 11 takes 3 hours. Total 8. Speed 3 would take 9 hours — too slow.
Must eat the biggest pile in one hour — that requires speed ≥ 30.
With 6 hours, speed 23 just barely works.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Classic 'binary search on the answer' problem. The answer (speed k) is between 1 and max(piles). For each candidate speed, you can FEASIBILITY-CHECK in O(n) by computing how many hours it takes. Then binary search the smallest k that's feasible. Don't search the array — search the SPACE OF ANSWERS.

clarifying_questions.question: Is the answer always between 1 and max(piles)?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Speed 1 is the slowest (always feasible if h is large enough). max(piles) is the fastest needed (one pile per hour). Confirms the binary search range.

clarifying_questions.question: Can Koko split piles? I.e., eat from two piles in the same hour?
clarifying_questions.why: No. Spec says each hour she eats from ONE pile. If pile < k, she eats it and stops (doesn't move to another).

clarifying_questions.question: Time-per-pile calculation — ceiling division?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes. Pile of 7 at speed 3 takes ceil(7/3) = 3 hours. Use math.ceil or (pile + k - 1) // k.

clarifying_questions.question: What if h is very large?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, h up to 10^9. Doesn't affect the algorithm — k is bounded by max(piles) ≤ 10^9, log of that is ~30 binary search steps. Each step is O(n). Total O(n log max).

clarifying_questions.question: Edge case: h equals number of piles?
clarifying_questions.why: Then Koko must eat each pile in exactly 1 hour. Speed = max(piles) is the answer.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Brute force: try every speed k from 1 to max(piles). For each, check if total hours ≤ h. O(max × n) — too slow when max is 10^9. Insight: if speed k works, speed k+1 also works (monotonic). So binary search on k. lo=1, hi=max(piles). Mid = (lo+hi)//2. Check feasibility in O(n). If feasible, try smaller (hi = mid). Else need faster (lo = mid+1). Total O(n log max).

edge_cases.details: Must eat each pile in 1 hour. Answer = max(piles).
Tons of time. Answer = 1 (slowest speed works).
pile=6, k=3: ceil(6/3)=2. pile=7, k=3: ceil(7/3)=3, not 2.33. Always round UP.
If k > max(piles), each pile takes exactly 1 hour. Same result as k = max(piles).

common_mistakes: Using regular division (pile // k) instead of ceiling — undercounts hours.
Searching the wrong space — trying to binary search on the piles array, not on speed.
Off-by-one: lo=0 (Koko must eat — speed 0 is impossible) or hi=max+1 (unnecessary).
While loop termination: lo<hi vs lo<=hi changes how 'answer' is captured.
Forgetting that ceil(pile/k) = (pile + k - 1) // k in integer arithmetic.

faang.introduction: Binary search on the answer. Find the minimum eating speed Koko needs to finish all bananas in h hours.
faang.introduction_context: Tests the 'binary search on a non-array' pattern. Common at FAANG. Sets up Capacity to Ship Packages, Find K-th Smallest, and other 'binary search on monotonic feasibility' problems.
really_testing: Recognition that 'find minimum X such that property holds' is binary-search-on-answer.
Defining the search space correctly (1 to max(piles)).
Writing a clean feasibility check (O(n) given a speed).
Ceiling division correctness.

rubric.criteria: Immediately recognizes binary search on the answer. States the monotonic property (faster speed → less time). Codes feasibility check with ceiling division. Tests carefully. ~12 min.
Reaches binary search after stating brute force. May need prompting on ceiling division.
Tries linear scan over speeds (O(max × n) — too slow). Or off-by-one in the binary search.
Can't recognize this as a search-on-answer problem. Tries to apply a totally different approach.

playbook: Restate the problem. Confirm one-pile-per-hour rule.
Ask about ceiling vs floor division.
Recognize: 'find min X such that feasibility(X)' = binary search on the answer space.
Define search range: lo=1, hi=max(piles).
Articulate monotonicity: if speed k works (hours ≤ h), so does k+1.
Write feasibility(k): sum(ceil(p/k) for p in piles) <= h.
Binary search: while lo < hi: mid = (lo+hi)//2; if feasibility(mid): hi = mid; else: lo = mid+1. Return lo.
Test on example: piles=[3,6,7,11], h=8. lo=1, hi=11. Binary search finds 4.
Test edge case: piles=[30,...], h=number_of_piles → max(piles).
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-15 min. Common follow-ups: Capacity to Ship Packages Within D Days, Split Array Largest Sum.
dont_do: Don't use floor division — undercounts hours.
Don't linear-scan all speeds 1 to max — O(max × n) is way too slow.
Don't search the wrong space (the piles array, not speed).
Don't forget to handle the 'no smaller speed works' case — must return some k.
Don't off-by-one the binary search bounds — use lo<hi for converge-to-answer.

approaches.name: Linear Scan (Brute Force)
approaches.explanation: Try every speed from 1 upward. The first feasible speed is the answer. O(max(piles) × n) — too slow when max(piles) is 10^9.

approaches.name: Binary Search on Answer
approaches.explanation: Binary search on the speed itself. The feasibility check (does speed k finish in ≤ h hours?) is monotonic — if k works, k+1 also works. Standard 'find smallest k where condition holds' binary search. O(log max) iterations, each O(n) for the feasibility check.

title: Subsets
summary: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution set must not contain duplicate subsets. Return the solution in any order.
constraints: All the numbers of nums are unique.

examples.explanation: All 2^3 = 8 subsets of {1,2,3}.
Empty set and the single-element set.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Power set. For each element, the choice is binary: include it or skip it. That gives 2^n total subsets. Three classic ways to generate them: recursive backtracking (the textbook answer), iterative 'build by extending existing subsets', or bit-manipulation (each int 0..2^n-1 is a subset mask). Backtracking is the most generalizable and the typical FAANG expected answer.

clarifying_questions.question: Are duplicate elements allowed in input?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says unique. If duplicates were allowed, you'd need extra dedup logic (Subsets II variant). Knowing the variant matters.

clarifying_questions.question: Output order — does it matter?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says any order. Saves the cost of sorting. Different algorithms naturally produce different orders.

clarifying_questions.question: Empty subset — should I include it?
clarifying_questions.why: Yes — the empty set is always part of the power set. Standard contract but worth confirming.

clarifying_questions.question: How big is n?
clarifying_questions.why: Constraint: n ≤ 10. So 2^n ≤ 1024 — small. Even O(2^n × n) is fast. No need for sub-exponential. Just be correct.

clarifying_questions.question: Are mutated subsets shared between iterations?
clarifying_questions.why: If you append the same list to the result repeatedly, all entries point to the same object. Make sure to append a COPY (result.append(current[:])). Classic bug.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Backtracking framing: at each index, two choices — include nums[i] in the current subset, or skip it. Recurse. When index reaches n, record the current subset (as a COPY). The recursion tree has 2^n leaves, each a distinct subset.

edge_cases.details: [] → [[]]. Just the empty subset itself. Recursion terminates immediately.
[5] → [[], [5]]. Two subsets — empty and full.
If you forget to copy `current` when appending, all 2^n results will be the same final list. Test by checking len(set(map(tuple, result))) == 2^n.
2^10 = 1024 subsets. Should run in milliseconds. If your code times out, you have an algorithmic bug.

common_mistakes: Appending `current` directly to result (shared reference) instead of `current[:]` (copy). Classic Python bug.
Forgetting to pop after recursing — leaves stale elements in `current` for subsequent calls.
Off-by-one on the recursion index — starting at 0 vs i+1 changes the semantics.
Trying to use sets — but subsets in Python are lists, and you'd need tuples for hashability.
Generating duplicates because you don't track which element-positions you've already considered.

faang.introduction: Generate all subsets of an array. Classic backtracking — the gateway problem to the entire backtracking pattern.
faang.introduction_context: Tests the choose-or-don't recursive pattern. Sets up follow-ups in Subsets II (with duplicates), Combinations (size-k subsets), Permutations.
really_testing: Recursive backtracking pattern recognition.
Correct state management: include → recurse → undo (the backtrack step).
Awareness of the shared-reference / copy-before-append bug.
Comfort with O(2^n) being acceptable when n is small.

rubric.criteria: Proposes backtracking immediately. Articulates 'include or skip' decision. Correctly copies the current list when appending. Walks through tree mentally for [1,2,3]. ~8 min.
Reaches backtracking. Initial version has the shared-reference bug; catches it after testing. Correctness in 12 min.
Uses iterative approach without explanation, or backtracking that produces duplicates. Off-by-one on the index.
Can't structure the recursion. Doesn't recognize the choose-or-skip pattern.

playbook: Restate: 'Generate all 2^n subsets of nums.'
Confirm any-order output and that input is duplicate-free.
Propose backtracking: 'At index i, choose to include nums[i] or skip. Recurse. When i == n, record current subset.'
Code: def backtrack(start, current): result.append(current[:]); for i in range(start, n): current.append(nums[i]); backtrack(i+1, current); current.pop().
Note the critical `current[:]` — append a COPY, not a reference.
Test on [1] → should produce [[], [1]].
Mention iterative 'add to existing' alternative and bit-manipulation, but say backtracking is the most generalizable.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Follow-ups: Subsets II (with duplicates, need to sort + skip), Combinations (only size-k), Permutations (order matters).
dont_do: Don't append current directly — append current[:] (a copy).
Don't forget the pop after recursion — leaves stale state for siblings.
Don't use a set to dedupe — input is guaranteed unique. Adding a set check signals you didn't read the spec.
Don't use bit manipulation as your primary answer if asked about backtracking — show the right tool for the right interview signal.
Don't fail to record the empty subset at the START of backtracking (record at every node, not just leaves).

approaches.name: Iterative — Add to Existing
approaches.explanation: Start with the empty subset. For each new num, take every existing subset and create a new subset with num appended. Doubles the result set each iteration. O(n) iterations × O(2^n) work per iteration = O(n × 2^n) total.

approaches.name: Backtracking
approaches.code:
def subsets(nums):
    result = []
    current = []
    def backtrack(start):
        result.append(current[:])  # COPY — critical
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            current.append(nums[i])
            backtrack(i + 1)
            current.pop()
    backtrack(0)
    return result
approaches.explanation: Recursive choose-or-skip. At each call, record the current subset (as a COPY), then for each remaining element, include it and recurse. The pop() at the end is the 'backtrack' — undoes the choice so the next iteration can try a different one. Visits each of 2^n subsets exactly once.

approaches.name: Bit Manipulation
approaches.code:
def subsets(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    result = []
    for mask in range(1 << n):
        subset = [nums[i] for i in range(n) if mask & (1 << i)]
        result.append(subset)
    return result
approaches.explanation: Each integer mask from 0 to 2^n - 1 represents a subset: bit i is 1 iff nums[i] is included. Iterate through all 2^n masks, build each subset by checking bits. Clean and compact but less generalizable than backtracking (e.g., harder to add early-termination).

title: Permutations
summary: Given an array nums of distinct integers, return all the possible permutations. You can return the answer in any order.
constraints: All the integers of nums are unique.

examples.explanation: All 3! = 6 orderings of [1,2,3].
2! = 2 orderings.
Single element — one permutation.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Generate all n! orderings of nums. Classic backtracking. At each step, pick any unused element, add to current permutation, recurse, then unpick. Track 'used' via a set or by swapping in place.

clarifying_questions.question: Are elements distinct or could there be duplicates?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says distinct. If duplicates existed, you'd need to skip duplicate branches (Permutations II variant). Important distinction.

clarifying_questions.question: Output order — does it matter?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says any order. Saves work.

clarifying_questions.question: How large is n?
clarifying_questions.why: Up to 6. n! = 720 max — very small. No optimization needed beyond correctness.

clarifying_questions.question: Should I generate permutations in lexicographic order?
clarifying_questions.why: Not required. But there's a classic 'Next Permutation' problem if lex order matters.

clarifying_questions.question: In-place mutation vs build-new-list?
clarifying_questions.why: Both work. Swapping in place is O(1) per step; building a new list is O(n) per step. Either is fine for n=6.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Backtracking: at each level of recursion, try every available number. Use a 'used' set or boolean array to track which are in the current permutation. When the current permutation has length n, record it. Then backtrack — pop the last element and try the next option.

edge_cases.details: [1] → [[1]]. Single permutation.
Spec says n ≥ 1, but defensive: [] → [[]] (the empty permutation).
[-1,-2,-3] — algorithm is value-agnostic, works fine.

common_mistakes: Appending `current` directly instead of `current[:]` (shared reference bug — all results become the same final permutation).
Forgetting to mark used/unused around the recursive call.
Off-by-one on the recursion depth (n vs n-1).
Trying to dedupe when input has unique elements — wasted check.
Confusing permutations with combinations or subsets (different problems).

faang.introduction: Backtracking essentials. Generate all permutations of a distinct-integer array.
faang.introduction_context: Often paired with Subsets and Combinations as the 'backtracking trio'. Tests the same pattern (choose-recurse-unchoose) with the twist that order matters.
really_testing: Backtracking template recall (choose-recurse-unchoose).
Correct state management — track which elements are used.
Awareness of the COPY bug (append current[:], not current).
Understanding that permutations differ from subsets/combinations (order matters).

rubric.criteria: Proposes backtracking immediately. Clean code with 'used' tracking. Correctly copies current. Walks through tree mentally. ~8 min.
Reaches backtracking. Has the shared-reference bug initially; catches it after testing.
Tries iterative without justification. Or backtracking with off-by-one. Or generates duplicates.
Can't structure the recursion. Confuses permutations with subsets.

playbook: Restate: 'Generate all n! orderings.'
Confirm distinct elements, any order output.
Propose backtracking with 'used' tracking.
Code: result=[]; current=[]; used=[False]*n.
Recurse: if len(current) == n, append current[:]. Else for each i: if not used[i]: used[i]=True; current.append(nums[i]); recurse; current.pop(); used[i]=False.
Optionally show the in-place swap variant.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 8-12 min. Common follow-ups: Permutations II (with duplicates — need to sort + skip), Next Permutation (single-step in lex order).
dont_do: Don't append current directly — copy via current[:].
Don't forget to unmark used after recursion (the 'unchoose' step).
Don't dedupe when input is unique — wasted code.
Don't generate subsets instead of permutations — they're different.
Don't use itertools.permutations() as your answer — shows library knowledge but not algorithm understanding.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Used Array
approaches.code:
def permute(nums):
    result = []
    current = []
    used = [False] * len(nums)
    def backtrack():
        if len(current) == len(nums):
            result.append(current[:])
            return
        for i in range(len(nums)):
            if used[i]:
                continue
            used[i] = True
            current.append(nums[i])
            backtrack()
            current.pop()
            used[i] = False
    backtrack()
    return result
approaches.explanation: Classic backtracking. Maintain a 'used' array. At each recursion level, try every unused element. Mark used → recurse → unmark. When current is full, record a COPY (current[:]). Each of n! permutations is built once.

approaches.name: Backtracking with In-Place Swap
approaches.explanation: Instead of using an extra 'used' array, swap elements in place. At depth d, fix position d by swapping in each remaining element. Recurse for position d+1. Swap back to undo. More memory-efficient but slightly harder to read.

title: Combinations
summary: Given two integers n and k, return all possible combinations of k numbers chosen from the range [1, n]. You may return the answer in any order.

examples.explanation: All C(4,2)=6 size-2 combinations of {1,2,3,4}.
Only one combination: pick the single element.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: Subsets-of-size-k variant. Backtracking: at each step, pick the next number from the remaining range. Track the start index to avoid duplicate combinations (since order doesn't matter, only pick increasing sequences).

clarifying_questions.question: Order within combinations — does it matter?
clarifying_questions.why: Combinations are unordered ([1,2] == [2,1]). To avoid duplicates, generate in increasing order. Confirms the start-index pattern.

clarifying_questions.question: Output order — does it matter?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says any order. Saves work.

clarifying_questions.question: Can the same number appear twice in a combination?
clarifying_questions.why: No — combinations are choices WITHOUT replacement. {1,1,2} isn't valid.

clarifying_questions.question: What's the upper bound on n?
clarifying_questions.why: n ≤ 20, k ≤ n. C(20,10) = 184756 — feasible. Worth confirming for time budget.

clarifying_questions.question: Is the empty combination (k=0) possible?
clarifying_questions.why: Per constraints, k ≥ 1. So no. But defensive: k=0 should return [[]].

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Backtracking, like Subsets but constrained to exact size k. Track a start index (the smallest number we can still pick). At each step, pick a number from [start, n], add to current, recurse with start+1. When current has length k, record it. Pruning: if current has c numbers and you need k-c more, no point continuing if there aren't k-c numbers left in [start, n].

edge_cases.details: n=3, k=1 → [[1], [2], [3]]. Singleton combinations.
n=3, k=3 → [[1,2,3]]. Only one combination — the full set.
[[]] — just the empty combination. Defensive code can handle this.
n=20, k=10 → 184756 combinations. Pruning helps but exponential growth is fundamental.
With pruning, recursion stops when remaining numbers < remaining slots. Significant speedup at the limits.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to copy current[:] when appending (shared-reference bug).
Not tracking start index — produces duplicates like [1,2] and [2,1].
Off-by-one on the range (n vs n+1 — must include n).
Forgetting the pop() after recursion — leaves stale state.
Not pruning when remaining numbers < remaining slots — works correctly but slower.

faang.introduction: Backtracking variant. Generate all size-k combinations of integers from 1 to n.
faang.introduction_context: Cousin of Subsets. Tests the 'start index' technique to avoid duplicates in combinatorial generation.
really_testing: Backtracking with constraints (target size k).
Using start index to avoid duplicate combinations.
Optional pruning when remaining < required.
Distinguishing combinations from permutations.

rubric.criteria: Recognizes as Subsets-of-size-k. Uses start index correctly. Adds pruning. Codes cleanly. ~8 min.
Backtracking works without pruning. Correct output.
Produces duplicates (missing start index). Or buggy recursion.
Can't structure the recursion. Confuses with permutations.

playbook: Restate: 'all size-k combinations from [1, n]'.
Confirm: combinations (unordered), no replacement.
Propose backtracking with start index (only increasing sequences).
Code: result=[]; current=[]; def backtrack(start): if len(current)==k: result.append(current[:]); return; for i in range(start, n+1): current.append(i); backtrack(i+1); current.pop(). backtrack(1).
Optional: add pruning — if (k - len(current)) > (n - start + 1), no point continuing.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 7-10 min. Follow-up: Combination Sum (with repetition).
dont_do: Don't generate permutations of size k — those are larger and duplicate.
Don't forget the start index — produces duplicates.
Don't forget current[:] copy.
Don't forget the +1 on range(start, n+1) — must include n.
Don't fail to pop after recursion.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Start Index
approaches.explanation: Standard backtracking. The `start` parameter ensures we only pick increasing sequences, avoiding duplicates like [1,2] and [2,1]. When current reaches size k, record a copy. Recursion depth is k; total work is O(C(n,k) × k).

approaches.name: Backtracking with Pruning
approaches.time: O(C(n,k) × k) but faster in practice
approaches.code:
def combine(n, k):
    result = []
    current = []
    def backtrack(start):
        if len(current) == k:
            result.append(current[:])
            return
        # Pruning: if remaining slots > remaining numbers, no valid combinations
        needed = k - len(current)
        for i in range(start, n - needed + 2):
            current.append(i)
            backtrack(i + 1)
            current.pop()
    backtrack(1)
    return result
approaches.explanation: Same algorithm with one pruning trick: at each level, we only need to consider numbers up to n - needed + 1 (where 'needed' is how many more we must pick). Picking a larger start would leave insufficient numbers to complete the combination. Saves significant work near the leaves.

title: Word Search
summary: Given an m x n grid of characters board and a string word, return true if word exists in the grid. The word can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically neighboring). The same letter cell may not be used more than once.
constraints: board and word consist of only lowercase and uppercase English letters.

examples.explanation: B(0,1) can only be used once. After A(0,0)→B(0,1)→C(0,2), there's no way back to a different B.

interview_tips.talk_through_it: DFS from every starting cell. For each path, mark visited cells (temporarily replace the char or use a separate set). When the path matches the word, return True. When it fails, backtrack (restore the cell). The 'same letter cell may not be used twice' constraint is what forces backtracking — without it, a simple existence-of-each-letter check would suffice.

clarifying_questions.question: Can cells be reused?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec explicitly says NO — same cell can't appear twice in a path. This is what makes backtracking necessary.

clarifying_questions.question: What does 'adjacent' mean — 4 directions or 8?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says horizontally or vertically — 4 directions (up/down/left/right). Diagonal is excluded.

clarifying_questions.question: Case sensitive?
clarifying_questions.why: Spec says both cases, treat as distinct. 'A' and 'a' are different.

clarifying_questions.question: Word length vs grid size?
clarifying_questions.why: Word up to 15, grid up to 6×6=36 cells. If word > 36, return false immediately. Worth confirming for early exit.

clarifying_questions.question: Is mutating the board OK?
clarifying_questions.why: If yes, you can use the board itself as the visited tracker (temporarily replace cell with '#' during recursion). Saves O(m×n) space. Most interviewers expect this for senior signal.

interview_tips.think_out_loud: Backtracking from each cell. For each (i,j), try to match word[0] there, then recursively try to match word[1] in adjacent cells, etc. Mark visited cells to prevent reuse. Restore on backtrack. Pruning: skip if board[i][j] != word[k] immediately. Worst case O(m × n × 4^L) where L is word length, but typically much less due to pruning.

edge_cases.details: board=[['A']], word='A' → true. Match at start, word fully consumed.
board=[['A']], word='B' → false.
If word.length > m*n, can't possibly fit (can't reuse cells). Early exit.
Must search ALL 4 directions, not just forward.
Like 'XYZ' on a grid with only 'A's. DFS prunes quickly via the first-char check.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to mark cell as visited before recursing → infinite loop or wrong answer on cycles.
Forgetting to UNMARK cell after backtracking → cells stay 'used' across different paths from different starts.
Using a separate visited set when modifying the board would work — costs extra O(m×n) space.
Off-by-one on the word index (k vs k+1).
Not pruning early when board[i][j] != word[k] — runs the recursion needlessly.

faang.introduction: Word Search. Given a grid and a word, can the word be formed by following adjacent cells (no reuse)?
faang.introduction_context: Classic 2D backtracking. Common at FAANG. Tests DFS on a grid + state restoration. Often a follow-up: 'Word Search II' generalizes to many words with a Trie.
really_testing: DFS on a 2D grid (handle bounds, 4 directions).
Backtracking discipline (mark, recurse, unmark).
In-place visited tracking (the senior optimization).
Pruning at the first character mismatch for performance.

rubric.criteria: Articulates DFS + backtracking. Uses in-place marking (modify board). Prunes on char mismatch. Clean code. ~12 min.
Reaches the algorithm with an external visited set. Code correct but uses extra space.
Forgets to unmark cells on backtrack. Or doesn't try all 4 directions. Or buggy bounds.
Can't structure the recursion. Can't handle grid traversal correctly.

playbook: Restate: 'Find word in grid by following adjacent cells, no reuse.'
Confirm: 4-direction adjacency, no cell reuse, case sensitive.
Propose backtracking DFS from each starting cell.
Sketch: for each (i,j), call dfs(i,j,0). dfs(i,j,k) returns True if word[k:] starts at (i,j).
Inside dfs: bounds check, mismatch check, mark, recurse to 4 neighbors with k+1, unmark, return any-success.
Show in-place marking: temporarily set board[i][j] = '#' (or any non-letter char) during recursion. Restore on backtrack.
Test on the example. Trace 'ABCCED' to confirm the path.
Discuss complexity: O(m×n × 4^L) worst case, typically way less due to pruning.
faang.playbook_meta: Total time: 12-18 min. Follow-up: Word Search II (multiple words via Trie).
dont_do: Don't forget to unmark cells on backtrack — leaves stale state.
Don't search only 1-2 directions — must try all 4.
Don't use an extra m×n visited matrix when in-place marking works.
Don't return early on first cell mismatch without trying the recursion — but DO prune the first char.
Don't try to be clever with global state — keep recursion pure.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Extra Visited Set
approaches.space: O(m × n) for visited + O(L) recursion
approaches.explanation: DFS from each cell. Use a 'visited' set to prevent reuse. Mark on entry, unmark on backtrack. Try all 4 directions. The 4^L term is the recursion fanout; m × n is the number of starting cells. Pruning (the char mismatch check) significantly reduces actual runtime.

approaches.name: Backtracking with In-Place Marking
approaches.explanation: Instead of a separate visited set, temporarily replace each visited cell with a sentinel char (e.g., '#' — anything that can't match a word letter). Restore on backtrack. Saves O(m×n) space. The senior signal — interviewers like seeing the in-place optimization.
